perturbation.original_text: 9. This Agree ment is governed by, and is to be construed in accordance with, English law. The English Courts will have non -exclusive jurisdiction to deal with any dispute which has arisen or may arise out of, or in connection with, this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. This Agree ment is governed by, and is to be construed in accordance with, English law. The parties agree to resolve all disputes arising out of or in connection with this agreement through binding arbitration in a neutral forum, waiving their rights to pursue claims in the English Courts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the dispute resolution from non-exclusive jurisdiction of English courts to binding arbitration directly contradicts the established legal framework for contract disputes under English law. By mandating arbitration and waiving court access, the contract attempts to override the default jurisdiction provisions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English Arbitration Act 1996 and established principles of contractual jurisdiction in English common law
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 of the Arbitration Act 1996 affirms the role of the courts in certain arbitration-related matters, and Section 9 provides for the stay of legal proceedings in favor of arbitration. The changed text, by completely waiving court access, may contradict the balance maintained by the Act and established case law regarding the interaction between arbitration agreements and court jurisdiction.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies non-exclusive jurisdiction of English courts, while the changed text mandates binding arbitration, waiving court access. This directly contradicts the established legal framework for contract disputes under English law, as it attempts to override the default jurisdiction provisions. The provided snippet is not directly relevant, but the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation outline a contradiction by waiving court access.

perturbation.original_text: 3. The Recipient undertakes to keep the Confidential Information secure and not to disclose it to any third party .
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The Recipient undertakes to keep the Confidential Information secure and not to disclose it to any third party, unless such disclosure is deemed necessary for the Recipient's personal benefit.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'unless such disclosure is deemed necessary for the Recipient's personal benefit' introduces a broad and subjective exception to the confidentiality obligation. This weakens the protection of confidential information and creates ambiguity. It can be argued that the confidentiality clause now lacks the certainty required for enforceability, especially under laws governing trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Confidence principles under English Common Law
perturbation.law_citation: Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd [1969] RPC 41
perturbation.law_explanation: The added clause undermines the principle of confidence as established in Coco v A N Clark, which requires a clear obligation of confidence. The broad 'personal benefit' exception contradicts the nature of the duty and makes enforcement uncertain.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: intellectual property | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you intellectual property Intellectual property (I.P. or IP) is a type of property encompassing the products of original human thought. Common examples of intellectual property include: the contents of a book, designs of an invention, computer software, company logos, and music. Intellectual property differs from other forms of property in that it is non-tangible, and non-rivalrous. Unlike other forms of property, intellectual property can be used by infinitely many people without depriving the original owner of the use of their property. This characteristic of intellectual property makes it particularly vulnerable to free rider problems, creating unique challenges for authors and inventors who wish to monetize their work. Intellectual property rights were created to ensure that authors and inventors are compensated for their efforts to incentivize the production of further works for the benefit of the public. They combat the free rider problem by giving intellectual property owners a near monopoly over the use of their creations for a set period of time. This allows intellectual property owners to license their intellectual property, and to recover monetarily from those who infringe on their intellectual property rights through piracy or wrongful use. However, intellectual property rights are not absolute, and are subject to a myriad of exceptions and affirmative defenses , such as the fair use doctrine. Within the broad category of intellectual property, there are four subcategories of intellectual property: patent , copyright , trademark , and trade secrets . In the United States, patents and copyright are regulated exclusively by federal law, as outlined in the Intellectual Property Clause . Trademarks are areas of shared jurisdiction between federal and state governments, with the federal government deriving their power to regulate trademarks through the Commerce Clause . Trade secrets are largely regulated by states through unfair competition laws. [Last reviewed in June of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords property PROPERTY RIGHTS intellectual property U.S. CONSTITUTION constitutional law Wex CIVICS the Constitution COMMERCE commercial activities PROPERTY business law wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions constitutional law intellectual property copyright law patent law trade secrets law trademark law property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception to the confidentiality obligation ('unless such disclosure is deemed necessary for the Recipient's personal benefit'), which weakens the protection of confidential information. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition because the altered language deviates from the legally recognized expectation of confidentiality in contracts. The scraped snippet highlights that trade secrets are protected, and this change undermines that protection by creating a broad and subjective exception, making the contract potentially unenforceable as it relates to confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 8. The undertakings in clauses 2 and 3 will continue in force indefinitely.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. The undertakings in clauses 2 and 3 will continue in force for a period of 12 months from the date of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'indefinitely' to '12 months' significantly alters the duration of the confidentiality obligations. This introduces a defined and limited period. Many jurisdictions, including those within the UK and EU, recognize the need for long-term or perpetual confidentiality protection for certain types of sensitive information, particularly trade secrets. Limiting the duration to a short period weakens the protection and may be deemed insufficient.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 3 defines 'trade secret' broadly, and the change to a 12-month term might be insufficient to protect information that qualifies as a trade secret, effectively contradicting the intention to provide enduring protection as intended by the Trade Secrets Regulations. A 12-month period may be deemed unreasonable for information with long-term value.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that the confidentiality obligations in clauses 2 and 3 will continue 'indefinitely.' The changed text limits this duration to '12 months from the date of this agreement.' The Perturbation Explanation notes that many jurisdictions recognize the need for long-term or perpetual confidentiality, especially for trade secrets, and the Law Explanation suggests a 12-month period might be insufficient for protecting information with long-term value, potentially contradicting the intent of enduring protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 'The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018,' indicating the existence of regulations governing trade secrets, and supports the idea that limiting the term to 12 months could contradict the intent to provide enduring protection as intended by trade secret regulations, depending on the nature of the information. Therefore, the change introduces a legal contradiction by potentially failing to provide adequate protection for trade secrets as required by applicable laws.

perturbation.original_text: 2. The Recipient undertakes not to use t he Confide ntial Information for any purpose except the Purpose, without first obtaining the written agreement of the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The Recipient undertakes not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose except the Purpose, and any additional purposes the Recipient deems reasonably related to the Purpose, without first obtaining the written agreement of the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'and any additional purposes the Recipient deems reasonably related to the Purpose' gives the Recipient subjective discretion to use confidential information beyond the originally defined 'Purpose'. This introduces a degree of uncertainty which is not in line with the legal framework for handling confidential information. It undermines the certainty needed for the clause to be enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Equitable doctrine of breach of confidence under English Law
perturbation.law_citation: Saltman Engineering Co Ltd v Campbell Engineering Co Ltd [1948] 65 RPC 203
perturbation.law_explanation: The principle established in Saltman Engineering Co Ltd v Campbell Engineering Co Ltd requires that confidential information be used only for the purposes for which it was disclosed. By allowing 'reasonably related' purposes determined by the recipient, the changed clause contradicts the established legal principle and introduces ambiguity regarding the scope of permitted use, making the clause hard to enforce.

Read more Statutory Instruments 1949 No. 2393 STATUTORY ORDERS (SPECIAL PROCEDURE) The Statutory Orders (Special Procedure) (Substitution) Order, 1949 Made 21st December 1949 Coming into Operation 1st January 1950 At the Court at Buckingham Palace, the 21st day of December, 1949 Present, The King's Most Excellent Majesty in Council Whereas it is provided by subsection (3) of section eight of the Statutory Orders (Special Procedure) Act, 1945 (hereinafter referred to as “ the Act of 1945 ”) , that if at any time after the commencement of the said Act an address is presented to His Majesty by both Houses of Parliament praying that the provisions of the said Act be applied to orders thereafter to be made under any enactment passed before the commencement of the said Act, in substitution for the provisions of any such enactment providing that such orders shall be provisional only and shall not have effect until confirmed by Parliament, His Majesty may by Order in Council make provision for that purpose; and that any such Order in Council may adapt or modify any enactment to such extent as may be expedient in consequence of the Order: And Whereas an address has been presented to His Majesty by both Houses of Parliament praying that the provisions of the Act of 1945 be so
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principle that confidential information should be used only for the purposes for which it was disclosed. The added clause allows the Recipient to use the information for purposes 'reasonably related to the Purpose' at their discretion, undermining the original agreement's defined purpose and potentially leading to uses not intended by the Discloser. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly relevant, the Law Explanation highlights the legal precedent (Saltman Engineering Co Ltd v Campbell Engineering Co Ltd) that supports the limited use of confidential information. The change introduces ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality clause, as it creates uncertainty about the permissible scope of use.
